title: Grasshopper pie
subtitle: A chilled American mint cream pie with a chocolate biscuit base
excerpt: Grasshopper pie is a retro American no-bake filling: peppermint-flavoured cream set with white chocolate in a dark biscuit base. The name references the cocktail it is based on; the colour comes from the mint, and in its most vivid versions, a small amount of green food colouring.
sourceNotes: The grasshopper pie is an American dessert associated with the mid-twentieth century cocktail culture; the name derives from the grasshopper cocktail (creme de menthe, creme de cacao, and cream). This no-bake cream version stabilised with white chocolate follows the home-baking adaptation of the recipe.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze the set pie and serve semifreddo-style, sliced from frozen and left to soften for 10 minutes.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The pie can be made up to 2 days ahead and kept covered in the fridge.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan: 160 degrees C; only the biscuit base is baked

glossaryTerms.term: White chocolate as a cream stabiliser
glossaryTerms.definition: Melted white chocolate stirred into softly whipped cream sets the filling as it cools without the texture drawbacks of gelatin: there is no risk of rubbery patches or uneven setting, and the result is smooth and sliceable. The white chocolate must be melted gently and cooled to near room temperature before folding into the cream; adding hot chocolate to cold cream causes it to seize into hard lumps.

content.text: The filling of a grasshopper pie is peppermint cream stabilised with melted white chocolate.

attrs.term: White chocolate as a cream stabiliser

content.text: White chocolate as a cream stabiliser

produces a sliceable, mousse-like texture without gelatin. The biscuit base is a simple crushed chocolate digestive crust pressed into the tin and baked briefly. The green food colouring is optional; the colour is part of the identity of the dish in its American original form, but the flavour is the same without it.

items.prepNote: for a chocolate base with plain digestives
1 tsp; add more to taste, very carefully

content.text: Preheat the oven to 180 degrees C (conventional). Crush the biscuits to fine crumbs in a food processor or bag. Combine with the melted butter (and cocoa powder if using plain biscuits) and press firmly into the base and sides of a 23 cm springform tin. Bake for 10 minutes. Leave to cool completely.

Melt the white chocolate in a heatproof bowl over barely simmering water, stirring until smooth. Remove from the heat and leave to cool to room temperature. Beat the cream cheese and icing sugar together in a large bowl until smooth. Stir in the cooled melted white chocolate and the peppermint extract. Taste and adjust the peppermint if needed; it should be clearly minty but not medicinal. Separately, whip the cold cream to soft peaks. Fold the cream into the chocolate mixture in two additions, keeping as much volume as possible. Pour into the cooled biscuit case and smooth the top. Refrigerate for at least 4 hours until fully set before slicing.
